Statistical Interpretation
Key Statistics

Scored 26 (Peak)
Analysis
Distribution Shape
Score distribution skews toward the upper-middle band (24–26), with 10 personnel scoring exactly 26 — the single most common score. The unit shows moderate-to-good retention of basic doctrine.
Near-Pass Cluster (Scores 20–22)
10 personnel scored 20–22 — just 1–3 points below the passing threshold. These "borderline" cases represent the easiest group to convert into passing scores with targeted review sessions.
Critical Failure Zone (Scores 14–18)
5 personnel (11%) scored 14–18, indicating severe knowledge deficiency. Two of these are Corporals — an NCO-level failure requiring urgent individual coaching, not just group remediation.
Wide Score Spread Among Corporals
The Corporal rank group spans from 14 (Sayod Carlo l) to 28 (GALAMAY ALDRIN A) — a 14-point range within the same rank, pointing to deeply unequal preparation across the unit's largest group.

Critical Item Analysis
🚨
Q22 — Signal for "Enemy" (51.1%)
Only 23 of 45 answered correctly (Thumbs Down). Many selected "Two fingers to chin" or "Three fingers up." In a live operation, the wrong hand signal for an enemy sighting can delay or prevent an engagement call — directly endangering the team and mission.
● Priority 1 — Operational Safety Risk
⚠️
Q30 — When Tracking Ends (53.3%)
24 of 45 answered correctly. 11 personnel believe tracking ends "at nightfall" — a critical misconception that would allow an enemy to escape by simply waiting for dark. Tracking ends only upon contact or confirmation, not by time of day.
● Tactical Doctrine Failure
⚡
Q7 — Standard VCTT Composition (55.6%)
25 of 45 correct. The standard Visual Combat Tracking Team has 6 members. Many selected 5 or 4 — meaning poorly staffed teams could be deployed, leaving gaps in security and tracking coverage during actual operations.
● Team Organization Gap
🌿
Q2 — Easiest Terrain for Tracking (57.8%)
Secondary Jungle (correct) was confused with Primary Jungle by 33.3%. Secondary jungle's dense undergrowth preserves ground signs better. Misidentifying terrain leads to wrong tactical approaches and missed enemy sign in actual patrol environments.
● Pre-Mission Planning Risk

Analytical Conclusion
✅ Conceptual Foundation is SolidPersonnel demonstrate near-perfect recall of basic definitions, search procedures, and enemy assessment — confirming that foundational classroom instruction has been effective and well-retained.
⚠ Practical Application LagsA sharp drop in accuracy on signals, terrain analysis, and team doctrine reveals a theory-vs-practice gap. These skills require hands-on repetitive drills to ingrain — not more classroom lectures.
"Personnel can define Combat Tracking with near-perfect accuracy — but in the field, a wrong hand signal for 'Enemy' is the difference between a successful engagement and a compromised team."

Why the Corporal Group is the Priority Concern
Largest Group with Highest Failure Count
23 Corporals — 51% of the unit. 10 of them failed (43% failure rate within the group). This is the single largest concentration of failures and requires structural remediation, not just individual coaching.
NCO-Level Accountability Gap
Corporals are expected to lead Private and PFC-level subordinates in field operations. A 57% pass rate among this NCO group means that nearly half of team leaders cannot demonstrate sufficient Combat Tracking proficiency.
Wide Variance Signals Inconsistent Preparation
The 14-point spread (14 to 28) within the same rank indicates that some Corporals studied rigorously while others did not. This points to a lack of enforced preparation standards prior to the examination.
Slide 7 / 11
Commander's Assessment
Critical Findings
Six Data-Driven Observations on Unit Combat Tracking Readiness
🚨
Finding 1 — Silent Signals Are a Lethal Gap
Q22 (51.1%) and Q23 (73.3%) confirm that hand signal doctrine is the unit's single most dangerous knowledge gap. In live operations, a wrong "Enemy" or "Booby Trap" signal can directly cause casualties, compromise the team's position, or allow an enemy to escape. This cannot wait for the next training cycle.
● Priority 1 — Operational Safety Risk
⚠️
Finding 2 — Tracking Termination Doctrine is Unclear
46.7% of all personnel have an incorrect understanding of when a tracking mission ends (Q30: 53.3%). 24.4% believe it ends at nightfall — a misconception with direct tactical consequences. An enemy that knows to wait for darkness exploits exactly this gap.
● Tactical Doctrine Failure
📋
Finding 3 — VCTT Team Composition is Poorly Known
44.4% do not know the correct 6-member VCTT composition (Q7: 55.6%). This directly affects task organization planning. Teams fielded with 4 or 5 members instead of 6 will have reduced security and tracking capability from the outset of the mission.
● Org Planning Risk
🌿
Finding 4 — Terrain & Sign Classification Needs Work
Q2 (57.8%) and Q26 (60%) show confusion between Primary and Secondary Jungle environments and sign types. Terrain misidentification leads to the wrong tracking approach being selected, causing trackers to look for signs in the wrong places and miss the enemy's actual trail indicators.
● Pre-Mission Planning Risk
📊
Finding 5 — Rank Does Not Predict Proficiency
Pvt Amuray, Mico R. scored 27/30, outperforming 18 Corporals. Two Corporals (Sayod Carlo l, and one unnamed) scored only 14–16/30. The strongest predictor of performance is individual preparation — not rank. Personnel assignments to key tracker roles should be proficiency-based, not seniority-based.
● Personnel Assignment Insight
✅
Finding 6 — Conceptual Foundation is Solid
Q14, Q16, Q17 achieved 100% accuracy (all 45 respondents correct). Q1, Q8, Q11, Q18 exceeded 93%. The unit has a strong theoretical base — the failure is in practical signal execution, team-level doctrine, and terrain assessment, not in fundamental comprehension of what Combat Tracking is.
● Positive — Strong Base to Build On
⚡ Overall VerdictUnit is at ~62% readiness for CT operations. The gap is not conceptual — it is procedural, tactical, and signal-based. Targeted practical drills will yield faster improvement than additional classroom instruction.
Slide 8 / 11
Action Plan
Commander's Recommendations
Six Prioritized Actions to Achieve Full Unit Combat Tracking Proficiency
Priority 1 · Immediate (Week 1)
🔴 Silent Signals Intensive Drill
Mandatory 2-hour practical exercise on all CTT hand signals — "Enemy," "Booby Trap," "Sign Found," and directional signals. Use peer evaluation (not written test) to confirm correct execution. Repeat daily until 100% accuracy across the unit. Failing this by itself is grounds for disqualification from CT operations.
⚡ Immediate
Priority 2 · Immediate (Week 1–2)
🔴 Remedial Program — 17 Failed Personnel
All 17 who failed (below 23/30) must attend structured remedial instruction. Priority cases requiring individual coaching: Sayod Carlo l and Dinopol Rey D (both 14/30), and the unnamed Corporal (16/30). Re-examination must be conducted within 2 weeks. All results to be formally documented.
⚡ Immediate
Priority 3 · Short-Term (Week 2)
🟠 VCTT Doctrine Re-Brief
Conduct a structured lecture on: standard 6-member VCTT composition, ATL duties, mission termination conditions (Q30), and the Bracketing Method. Issue a laminated pocket reference card to all 45 personnel covering team roles, search drill types, and hand signal guide. Follow with a 10-item oral assessment.
📅 Short-Term
Priority 4 · Short-Term (Week 2–3)
🟠 Terrain Classification Workshop
Address Primary vs. Secondary Jungle confusion (Q2: 57.8%) and sign type misidentification (Q26: 60%). Conduct a terrain walk-through where possible, or photo-based identification exercises. Focus on why Secondary Jungle preserves ground signs better and how this alters tracking technique selection.
📅 Short-Term
Priority 5 · Mid-Term (Month 1–2)
🏆 Recognize & Leverage Top Performers
Formally recognize GALAMAY ALDRIN A (28/30) and Dalmino Ranel G, Cueva Greggy Insigne, Arazo orly d, and Amuray, Mico R. (all 27/30) with citations. Designate these five as peer instructors for the remedial cycle. Note that Pvt Amuray outperformed most Corporals — rank-blind excellence deserves recognition.
🎯 Mid-Term
Priority 6 · Long-Term (Ongoing)
📈 Monthly Proficiency Monitoring
Implement a monthly 10–15 item knowledge check focused on the weakest areas (signals, VCTT structure, terrain, mission end). Track trends over 6 months and report to the unit commander quarterly. Set unit target: ≥85% pass rate within 3 months. Replace written tests with scenario-based field evaluations as the primary proficiency metric.
🌱 Long-Term
📌 Overall Training StrategyTransition from classroom-only instruction to a scenario-based, peer-evaluated, repetitive drill model. Theoretical knowledge is adequate — tactical application, team signals, and procedures are the critical gaps. Field exercises with live evaluation must replace written tests as the primary proficiency standard going forward.
